title: Acute wheeze and chest tightness

chart.oneLiner: 24F with a history of asthma presents with several hours of progressive wheeze, cough, and chest tightness after a viral cold.

opening: My chest feels really tight and I can't stop wheezing — my inhaler isn't cutting it.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 24-year-old woman, anxious, speaking in short phrases between breaths, using her albuterol frequently. Cooperative.

HPI: Several hours of worsening wheeze, dry cough, and chest tightness, triggered by a head cold over the past 2 days. Albuterol giving only brief relief. Similar but milder episodes in the past. NO fever, NO purulent sputum, NO pleuritic chest pain, NO leg swelling/calf pain, NO known cardiac disease.
PMH: asthma (a prior ED visit, never intubated). MEDS: albuterol PRN, inhaled corticosteroid (admits poor adherence if asked). SOCIAL: nonsmoker, cat at home (trigger).

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: mild-moderate distress, speaking in phrases, mild accessory muscle use.
- Vitals: BP 124/76, HR 110, RR 28, 37.1°C, SpO2 93% RA.
- Lungs: diffuse expiratory WHEEZE bilaterally, prolonged expiratory phase, good air entry (air entry would be the worry if it dropped).
- Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmur. Extremities: no edema, no calf tenderness.
- Peak expiratory flow: reduced to ~50% of her personal best (if measured).

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the inhaled-steroid nonadherence or the cat unless asked about adherence/medications or triggers.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.CBC: WBC 8.0 with mild eosinophilia, Hgb 13.5, Plt 250.
labs.ABG: pH 7.46, PaCO2 32, PaO2 80 — respiratory alkalosis. (A NORMAL or RISING PaCO2 would be an ominous sign of fatigue.)

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus tachycardia, S1Q3T3, and T-wave inversions in V1-V3 (right heart strain).
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Sinus tachycardia, S1Q3T3, and T-wave inversions in V1-V3 (right heart strain). Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Hyperinflated lungs, flattened hemidiaphragms, increased retrosternal air, narrow cardiac shadow.
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Hyperinflated lungs, flattened hemidiaphragms, increased retrosternal air, narrow cardiac shadow. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential diagnosis (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Asthma exacerbation (leading,8; accepts acute asthma/reactive airways). Anaphylaxis (CANT-MISS to consider,4). Pulmonary embolism (CANT-MISS,4). COPD/bronchitis (core,3). Heart failure (less likely; core,2). Inhaled foreign body (bonus,2). Named leading dx w/ justification (core,2).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Asthma exacerbation. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Anaphylaxis; Pulmonary embolism. Also reasonable: COPD/bronchitis; Heart failure.

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial assessment/workup do you want? (Consider what changes management.)
steps.rubric: Peak expiratory flow / spirometry (CANT-MISS,5). Continuous pulse oximetry (3). ABG if severe or not improving (3). CXR if atypical features or to exclude complication (2). CBC (2). PENALTY: extensive cardiac/CT workup with no indication in a classic young asthmatic (-2).

penalties.item: extensive cardiac/CT workup with no indication in a classic young asthmatic

contraindicatedTests: extensive cardiac/CT workup with no indication in a classic young asthmatic

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia, no acute ischemia. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (7); no acute ischemic ST changes (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: over-calling ischemia (-3).
keyFindings: names S1Q3T3 and anterior T inversions
criticalFindings: Right heart strain pattern (S1Q3T3, anterior T-wave inversions).
commonMistakes: treating S1Q3T3 as diagnostic in isolation
steps.idealAnswer: Right heart strain: sinus tachycardia with S1Q3T3 and T-wave inversions in V1-V3.

criticalActions: Right heart strain pattern (S1Q3T3, anterior T-wave inversions).
coreActions: names S1Q3T3 and anterior T inversions

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = normal or mildly hyperinflated, no consolidation/effusion/pneumothorax. CREDIT: correctly identifies a normal/hyperinflated film (5); explicitly excludes pneumothorax and consolidation as complications (5). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
commonMistakes: counting rib spaces incorrectly and missing hyperinflation
steps.idealAnswer: Emphysematous hyperinflation: flattened hemidiaphragms, increased retrosternal lucency, >6 anterior ribs.

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: What is your leading diagnosis and how do you gauge severity?
steps.rubric: Asthma exacerbation (5). Uses PEF, work of breathing, and ABG to grade severity (3). Recognizes a normalizing/rising PaCO2 as a sign of impending respiratory failure (bonus,2).

bonusActions.item: Recognizes a normalizing/rising PaCO2 as a sign of impending respiratory failure

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Inhaled SABA, repeated/continuous (CANT-MISS,7). Add ipratropium for moderate-severe (4). Systemic corticosteroids early (6). Controlled supplemental O2 to keep SpO2 >92% (3). IV magnesium for severe/refractory (3). Reassess response with PEF/exam; escalate if deteriorating (2). PENALTY: discharging without reassessment or missing rising PaCO2 as respiratory fatigue (-3).

penalties.item: discharging without reassessment or missing rising PaCO2 as respiratory fatigue

unsafeActions: discharging without reassessment or missing rising PaCO2 as respiratory fatigue

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 24F with a history of asthma presents with several hours of progressive wheeze, cough, and chest tightness after a viral cold. The diagnosis is Asthma exacerbation, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmur. Extremities: no edema, no calf tenderness.
Lungs: diffuse expiratory WHEEZE bilaterally, prolonged expiratory phase, good air entry (air entry would be the worry if it dropped).

reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Asthma exacerbation while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Asthma exacerbation and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
commonPitfalls: discharging without reassessment or missing rising PaCO2 as respiratory fatigue
criticalInterpretationItems: Sinus tachycardia. No acute ischemic changes.
Normal or mild hyperinflation; NO consolidation, effusion, or pneumothorax. (CXR mainly to exclude complications.)
keyImageInterpretationFindings: Right heart strain pattern (S1Q3T3, anterior T-wave inversions).
criticalImageInterpretationFindings: Right heart strain pattern (S1Q3T3, anterior T-wave inversions).
imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
